title: Inversion: The Problem-Solving Technique Nobody Uses
summaryMarkdown:
## Core idea
The essay presents inversion as a deceptively simple problem-solving method: instead of asking how to succeed, ask how to guarantee failure and then avoid those conditions. This backward framing is powerful because the path to failure is usually clearer, shorter, and easier to act on than the path to ideal success.

## Why forward thinking often fails
Directly chasing success creates a wide field of attractive options, many of which feel productive without actually helping. Inversion collapses that field into a smaller set of obvious failure modes, which makes action easier because the mistakes are specific and identifiable.

## Example and applications
The health example shows the logic: rather than designing the perfect routine, identify a handful of behaviors that would reliably make someone unhealthy and refuse them. The essay extends this logic to investing, relationships, careers, and writing, arguing that avoiding known ruin often matters more than perfectly engineering upside.

## Discipline and rarity
Inversion sounds passive, but it requires steady, deliberate avoidance of easy and socially acceptable mistakes. The writer recommends a recurring 'stupidity audit' to identify the worst plausible moves in advance and argues that long-term advantage often comes less from brilliance than from consistently refusing obvious self-sabotage.
